title: Binary search
prompt: Write binary_search(sorted_list, target) returning the index of target, or -1 if absent. The list is sorted ascending. This is your first O(log n) algorithm.

details.restate: The list is already sorted, smallest to largest. Give back the POSITION of the target value in the list, or -1 if it isn't there at all.

io.because: 4 isn't in the list — -1 is the not-found signal

terms.meaning: arranged smallest to largest — the guarantee that makes halving the search possible

help.plain: The list is sorted, so you never look one-by-one. You check the middle: too big? throw away the right half. Too small? throw away the left half. Repeat on what's left.
help.analogy: Like finding a word in a paper dictionary — you open to the middle, see if your word comes before or after, and instantly ignore half the book. A few halvings and you're there.
help.mnemonic: MCH — Middle, Compare, Halve. Look at the Middle, Compare to the target, Halve the range toward the side it must be on.
help.nudge: You need two markers for the range you're still searching — a low and a high. What happens to each when the middle is too small?

example.problem: Guess a secret number between 1 and 100 in as few tries as possible, given 'higher'/'lower' hints (a different task).
example.note: Same halving engine: a low/high range, check the middle, move one edge inward. Yours returns the index it found (or -1) instead of a guess count.
